title: Aussie gov't tells volunteers to throw out thousands of functioning test routers
gist: Cisco-owned SamKnows remotely bricked thousands of still-functional OpenWRT-based broadband test routers after Australia's ACCC Measuring Broadband Australia program ended June 30, 2026, choosing avoidable e-waste over a firmware update that would have freed the devices for consumer reuse.

insights.claim: Thousands of SamKnows 'whitebox' routers deployed for Australia's Measuring Broadband Australia program were remotely disabled after June 30, 2026 once the ACCC program concluded, despite the hardware still functioning.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Illustrates vendor kill-switch risk: hardware tied to a service contract can be bricked at will, a governance concern for any managed-device deployment.
insights.quote: Last week, thousands of SamKnows routers were bricked after a government program ran its course.
insights.locator: Last week, thousands of SamKnows routers

insights.claim: The ACCC initially planned to release about 4,000 SamKnows whiteboxes and had distributed over 2,600 by December 2020, indicating the disabled fleet numbered in the low thousands.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Scales the e-waste footprint of a single vendor decision to thousands of units.
insights.locator: When asked by Ars, the ACCC didn't specify

insights.claim: The disabled whiteboxes run a custom version of OpenWRT and can be reflashed into fully functional Wi-Fi routers, meaning the bricking created e-waste that a final firmware update could have prevented.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Highlights the gap between vendor convenience and circular-economy/right-to-repair expectations that regulators increasingly scrutinize.
insights.quote: can easily be reflashed into normal Wi-Fi routers with very decent performance

insights.claim: A volunteer successfully reflashed their own whitebox into a working OpenWRT Wi-Fi router, an operation that required a soldering iron because the vendor withheld software support.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Shows a technical workaround exists, sharpening criticism that the bricking was a choice, not a necessity.
insights.quote: I personally reflashed my own device (an operation that requires a soldering iron when you do it without company support), and it is now working great as a Wi-Fi router running OpenWRT.

insights.claim: Cisco acquired SamKnows in 2023 and SamKnows.com now redirects to Cisco's ThousandEyes networking-intelligence unit, but Cisco deflected all inquiries to the ACCC, leaving the reason for bricking unexplained.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Shows how acquisitions absorb small measurement vendors into large networking incumbents, with accountability diffusing across contracts.
insights.quote: Per SamKnow's agreement with the ACCC, all inquiries related to the Measurement Broadband Australia program should be directed to the ACCC.

insights.claim: Stakeholders may have disabled the routers to avoid liability for future issues if the devices were updated for continued consumer use, per the author's speculation.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Liability-avoidance as the driver of e-waste is a recurring pattern worth watching in device end-of-life policy.
insights.quote: It's possible that SamKnows/Cisco and/or the ACCC don't want to be associated with future potential issues or concerns about the routers should the devices be updated for easy, continued use.

insights.claim: SamKnows previously distributed 9,000 routers for a 2011 US FCC broadband-speed study, and neither the FCC nor SamKnows has stated what happened to those devices afterward.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Suggests opaque device end-of-life handling is a longstanding pattern across government broadband-measurement programs.
insights.quote: SamKnows distributed 9,000 routers to volunteers to help test Internet speeds in the US for a report published in 2011.
